title: Horseradish cream
subtitle: Fresh-grated root folded into double cream — the roast beef companion
excerpt: Made from freshly grated horseradish folded into double cream with a little vinegar and sugar. It has a heat that prepared horseradish sauce rarely matches, and it takes five minutes.
sourceNotes: Classic British condiment, documented in Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, as a standard accompaniment to roast beef. Mrs Beeton's 1861 manual also carries the fresh-grated root version.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 hours ahead and refrigerate. The heat from the root intensifies as it sits — check the seasoning before serving.

content.text: Fresh horseradish root is unpredictably fiery in a way that the prepared sauce in a jar almost never is. The heat hits at the back of the throat rather than at the front of the mouth, which is why it works so well alongside a fatty, unctuous slice of rare beef. If you grow it, dig a root just before use; if you buy it, look in delis and farm shops rather than supermarkets, where the pre-grated kind tends to be mild.

Grate the horseradish away from your face and in a ventilated space. It releases volatile compounds that irritate the eyes more than an onion. The moment it is grated, the heat begins to fade — so grate it last, just before folding into the cream.

items.prepNote: freshly grated (about a 7 cm piece, peeled)

content.text: Pour {{double-cream}} of double cream into a small bowl and whisk to soft peaks. It should hold its shape loosely when the whisk is lifted, not stand in stiff points.

Peel and finely grate {{horseradish-fresh}} of fresh horseradish root on the fine side of a box grater. Work quickly. Fold the grated horseradish into the cream along with {{white-wine-vinegar}} of white wine vinegar, {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar, and the Dijon mustard if using. Season with salt. Taste: the cream should be sharp, hot, and lightly savoury.

items.cause: Root was old and its volatile compounds had mostly dissipated, or the root was grated too far in advance
items.fix: Use the prepared horseradish sauce from a jar as a fallback — add to taste. Fresh root should be used the day it is grated.

items.symptom: Cream has collapsed and is watery
items.cause: Over-whipped and then the whisk continued, or stored too long
items.fix: Fold in a spoonful of thick Greek yoghurt to restore body. The texture will be slightly different but the flavour holds.
Fold in more lightly whipped cream and a pinch of sugar.

items.cause: The grated horseradish oxidised before being folded in
items.fix: Grate the root directly into the cream next time, or use within 5 minutes of grating.

content.text: Horseradish with beef is a pairing found across northern and central Europe, but the cream version is distinctly British. Acton's 1845 recipe for 'horseradish sauce' is the same as this one: grated root, cream, vinegar, and a little sugar. Before refrigeration, a sauce this quick and made to order was a practical necessity — no preserving, no cooking, nothing to go wrong. It remains the most direct way to use the root and the one that shows off its heat most clearly.
